skill.name: azure-identity-ts
skill.description: Configure Azure Identity SDK authentication for TypeScript applications using DefaultAzureCredential, managed identity, service principals, certificates, and interactive login.
skill.summary: Configure Azure Identity authentication patterns for TypeScript and JavaScript Azure SDK clients.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The high static score is mostly false positive noise from Markdown code fences, Azure SDK credential class names, and example Azure endpoints. No prompt injection, obfuscation, credential exfiltration, or hidden executable payload was found. The skill still has medium risk because it teaches handling Azure secrets, certificate paths, CLI credentials, bearer tokens, and verbose logging.

medium_findings.title: Azure Secret and Certificate Handling Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill documents Azure tenant, client, secret, certificate path, certificate password, and federated token variables. This is expected for an Azure Identity skill, but users could mishandle secrets if examples are copied without secret management controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly names Azure credential environment variables and certificate values. The context is legitimate documentation, so the risk is handling exposure rather than malicious use.

medium_findings.title: Developer Credential Chain Uses Local Azure Tools
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends credential types that can use Azure CLI, Azure Developer CLI, and Azure PowerShell login state. This is normal SDK behavior, but it depends on local authenticated tooling and inherited permissions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Azure SDK credential classes are clearly present and can use local authenticated command-line state. No command injection or unauthorized execution is shown.

medium_findings.title: Verbose Azure Logging Example
medium_findings.description: The debugging section enables verbose Azure logging and forwards log arguments to console output. This may expose tokens, scopes, account names, or tenant details if used in production.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Verbose logging and console forwarding are explicitly documented. The exact sensitive fields depend on runtime SDK behavior and deployment context.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static Ruby backtick findings point to Markdown code fences and TypeScript examples. They are documentation blocks, not executable Ruby or shell code in the skill package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence lines are fenced documentation examples in SKILL.md. No runnable script file, subprocess call, or dynamic command construction is present.

low_findings.title: Azure Endpoint Examples Are SDK Configuration
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are Azure service endpoints or OAuth scopes used by SDK clients. They do not send credentials to an unknown host or unrelated external service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both locations are standard Azure service or scope strings in client configuration. No exfiltration destination or network request wrapper is defined by the skill.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Not Supported
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm finding references the skill metadata description. No MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or custom cryptographic implementation was found in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is descriptive metadata about Azure Identity authentication. It contains no weak algorithm name or cryptographic operation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Heuristic Combination Dismissed After Review
dangerous_patterns.description: The critical heuristic combined code fences, Azure endpoints, and credential examples. Manual review found no obfuscation, prompt injection, hidden network call, or credential exfiltration intent.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious combination exists only as documentation for Azure Identity usage. The file contains no hidden execution logic or data transmission code outside normal SDK examples.

content.value_statement: Azure authentication setup can be confusing across local development, containers, and cloud hosting. This skill gives practical TypeScript patterns for Azure Identity credentials and SDK clients.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install and import the Azure Identity SDK package.
Explains DefaultAzureCredential and its common credential chain order.
Provides TypeScript examples for managed identity and service principals.
Covers interactive browser, device code, CLI, PowerShell, and developer CLI credentials.
Demonstrates bearer token provider usage for Azure API scopes.
Lists TypeScript credential types and custom credential structure.
limitations: Does not validate Azure permissions, tenants, subscriptions, or role assignments.
Does not provide production secret storage or key rotation automation.
Examples use placeholders and require real Azure configuration before use.
Does not cover every Azure SDK client or every cloud compliance requirement.

use_cases.title: Set up local Azure SDK authentication
use_cases.description: Use DefaultAzureCredential with Azure CLI or developer CLI login for a TypeScript application.

use_cases.title: Move a service to managed identity
use_cases.description: Replace service principal secrets with managed identity patterns for Azure-hosted workloads.

use_cases.title: Document supported credential flows
use_cases.description: Compare service principal, certificate, device code, and sovereign cloud credential options.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a credential type
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose an Azure Identity credential for a TypeScript app running locally and in Azure App Service.

prompt_templates.title: Add DefaultAzureCredential
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to use DefaultAzureCredential with an Azure Storage client in a TypeScript project.

prompt_templates.title: Replace client secrets
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Azure authentication approach and suggest how to move from client secrets to managed identity.

prompt_templates.title: Design a credential chain
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a ChainedTokenCredential strategy for local development, Kubernetes workload identity, and production managed identity.

output_examples.input: I need authentication for a TypeScript service on Azure Functions.
output_examples.output: Recommendation: use DefaultAzureCredential first. Use Azure CLI locally and managed identity in Azure Functions with assigned roles.

output_examples.input: My team uses service principals today and wants fewer secrets.
output_examples.output: Migration plan: prefer managed identity for Azure-hosted services, use workload identity for Kubernetes, and keep service principals only where required.

output_examples.input: I need a token provider for an Azure AI endpoint.
output_examples.output: Suggested approach: create a bearer token provider with DefaultAzureCredential and the Azure Cognitive Services scope.

best_practices: Prefer DefaultAzureCredential for code that must run in both development and Azure hosting.
Use managed identity or workload identity before storing long-lived client secrets.
Limit verbose Azure logs to debugging sessions and remove sensitive output before sharing.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode tenant IDs, client IDs, secrets, tokens, or certificate passwords in source code.
Do not give managed identities broad roles when a narrower role is enough.
Do not rely on interactive login flows for unattended production services.

faq.question: Does this skill run Azure commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation and examples. Users must run any Azure CLI or login steps themselves.

faq.question: Is DefaultAzureCredential always the best option?
faq.answer: It is a strong default for many apps, but explicit credentials may be better for strict hosting or tenant requirements.

faq.question: Can this skill create Azure role assignments?
faq.answer: No. It explains authentication code patterns, not Azure resource provisioning or access control changes.

faq.question: Does it support managed identity?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes system-assigned and user-assigned managed identity examples.

faq.question: Does it cover sovereign clouds?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows the Azure Government and Azure China authority host options.

faq.question: How should secrets be handled?
faq.answer: Use managed identity when possible. When secrets are required, store them in approved secret managers or protected environment settings.
